pages.title: Ten Fifty Bakers | Off-Grid Luxury Retreat Tasmania

blocks.text: Off-Grid Wilderness Retreat in Tasmania
Step into a secluded sanctuary.
Hand-built from rich Tasmanian timbers, the retreat sits just 30 minutes from Devonport, perfectly placed for those who crave space, stillness and soul-deep rest.
Wilderness, Reimagined.
Here, the Tasmanian wilderness becomes your living room and every moment is curated for quiet, conscious indulgence.
A Story Etched in Timber and Time.
Built by hand in the 1980s, this retreat lay untouched for decades before its thoughtful revival. Ten Fifty Bakers blends authentic materials with a commitment to rare, meaningful stays.
A Secluded Tasmanian Sanctuary for the Discerning Traveller

More than a getaway, Ten Fifty Bakers is a private wilderness retreat in North West Tasmania designed for those who seek beauty that feels intimate, not staged. Luxury here is silence, space, and clean air. It’s walking barefoot to your bath under the stars, knowing you’re the only one for miles.

blocks.alt: Single-storey wooden house with metal roof and solar panels on a lawn, large windows, benches, outdoor seating, trees in background.
Living room with wood-panelled walls and floor, brown leather sofas, black bean bag, cowhide rug, large windows, black stove.
A person in a dressing gown sits on a wooden sauna edge; another person relaxes in an outdoor bath at sunset, trees in the background.

blocks.text: Your Stay, Your Pace.

Every element of Ten Fifty Bakers is crafted for presence, from the scent of warm timber as you step inside, to the golden light spilling across stone at dusk. Whether you spend your days hiking through our private trails, the adjoining Narawntapu National Park or reading by the fire, this is a place to recalibrate and savour the kind of time you can’t find anywhere else.
Some places feel entirely your own.
1,137 Acres
With exclusive use of the entire property.
Wildlife Encounters
Spot pademelons, native birds, and other Tasmanian species.
Stargazer’s Haven
Enjoy crystal-clear skies free from light pollution.
Seasonal Magic
Experience misty winter mornings and golden summer sunsets.
Outdoor Baths Under the Stars
Immerse yourself in steaming water as the night sky stretches endlessly overhead. No city lights. No interruptions. Just you, the wilderness, and the quiet hum of nature.
Panoramic Forest Sauna
Breathe in eucalyptus-tinged air while gazing out over treetops, valleys, and distant peaks.
1,137 Acres of Private Wilderness
Explore 20 kilometres of private trails, open spaces and hidden corners, all yours to wander.
Such a beautiful spot – couldn’t have been better.

pages.title: Exclusive Wilderness Accommodation | Ten Fifty Bakers

blocks.text: Exclusive Wilderness Accommodation in Tasmania
One property. One booking. One group at a time.
Ten Fifty Bakers isn’t just a place to stay, it’s the whole experience.
Designed for just one group at a time

From wildlife encounters at dawn to evenings spent around the fire pit, each moment at Ten Fifty Bakers feels both grounding and extraordinary. Here, luxury is not loud or staged, it’s the quiet confidence of space, stillness, and a stay that becomes your own story.Whether you come as a couple seeking romance and seclusion or to gather with friends and family in a larger group, you’ll never share the space. Every firelit evening, every starry bath, and every bush walk belongs only to you.

blocks.alt: A person holding a mug stands outdoors, out of focus, with sunlight shining through trees in the background.

blocks.text: Rates

All rates below are for up to four guests. Travelling with a larger group? The house comfortably sleeps up to 10 with 4 beds that convert from King Size to Singles and 1 Double Bed.Add additional guests for $100 per person per night.
A $300 cleaning fee applies per stay. The price includes full access to all amenities
Your Wilderness Awaits

Step straight into nature with more than 20 kilometres of private walking tracks winding through 1,137 acres of untouched Tasmanian wilderness. Choose from short strolls or longer trails through creeks, gullies and native bushland each revealing a different side of this ancient landscape.
Life Among the Wild

The land hums with life here. Spot Wedge-tailed Eagles circling overhead, Forester Kangaroos grazing in the distance and Echidnas shuffling through the undergrowth. With over 80 native plant species, every walk brings something new, a living snapshot of Tasmania as it has been for thousands of years.
Slow Living, Redefined

Soak in an outdoor bath beneath a canopy of stars. Breathe deeply in the panoramic sauna overlooking Narawantapu National Park. Inside, the hand-built retreat surrounds you with rich Tasmanian timbers, warmth and quiet. This is luxury that whispers, not shouts.
The Rhythm of the Seasons

Each visit tells a different story. Misty mornings fade into sunlight, golden afternoons linger on the hills and clear night skies are dusted with stars. Whether you come in summer or winter, every moment here feels like it belongs only to you.
Some places feel entirely your own.

pages.title: Our Story | The Rebirth of Ten Fifty Bakers

blocks.text: Our Story
A Tasmanian Story Etched in Timber and Time
Ten Fifty Bakers was built by hand in the 1980s as a grand act of love. For decades it sat untouched, hidden within 1,137 acres of wild Tasmanian wilderness, quietly waiting to be discovered again.
Luxury Rooted in Nature

The original structure at Ten Fifty Bakers was crafted from rich Tasmanian timbers, stone and materials drawn from the land itself. It was never just a building; it was a vision, a sanctuary created with intention and care.That foundation still stands today, every beam and detail carrying the spirit of those who built it. What began as an idea to keep it alive grew into something much bigger. Turning it into a retreat gave us the reason not just to preserve it, but to elevate it.

blocks.alt: Single-storey wooden house with metal roof and solar panels on a lawn, large windows, benches, outdoor seating, trees in background.
Living room with wood-panelled walls and floor, brown leather sofas, black bean bag, cowhide rug, large windows, black stove.
A person in a dressing gown sits on a wooden sauna edge; another person relaxes in an outdoor bath at sunset, trees in the background.
A person in a dressing gown stands on the veranda of a wooden house, looking out towards the garden in early morning light.

blocks.text: From Forgotten to Rare Retreat

For years the property sat in stillness, untouched by time. During that pause the land reclaimed its voice, wildlife returned, forests thickened and silence deepened.When we found the house, it was not falling apart, but it was frozen in time. Built with love in the 1980s, it needed more than a coat of paint. It needed a future.The idea of opening it as a retreat gave it new purpose. What began as a simple renovation soon became something greater. Inside, the surfaces were renewed, with fresh paint, restored plumbing and timber floors brought back to life.
A Sanctuary Restored

The transformation reached beyond the house itself. The land was cleared and revived, with one hundred truckloads of gravel shaping new driveways and paths. Trees were removed to reopen the views remembered from the 1980s, leaving eleven enormous piles of trunks and branches to burn. Thirty truckloads of bark and twenty of topsoil gave the property space to breathe again.Ten Fifty is off-grid but never off limits. Comfort was built in from the start, with a quiet solar system, battery storage and a backup generator to keep everything running. Hot water flows when you need it, the kitchen is ready for real cooking and Starlink internet is available if you want to stay connected.Water is drawn from a 22-metre underground bore, naturally filtered through sand, filling both the outdoor baths and the main house. No electricity mains and water supported by a 90,000 litre tank and private bore, just pure self-sustained living, complete with proper flushing toilets, because even in the middle of nowhere, the little things matter.

pages.title: Explore Northern Tasmania | Ten Fifty Bakers

blocks.text: Explore Northern Tasmania from your private retreat
A Sanctuary with the best of Tasmania at your doorstep
Ten Fifty Bakers feels a world away yet sits perfectly positioned to explore northern Tasmania’s wineries, dining, and natural attractions.
The perfect base for discovery

From Tamar Valley cellar doors and Launceston’s UNESCO-recognised food scene, down to Northwest coastlines and hidden bush trails, this region marries rugged nature with refined experience, a place where you can taste fine wine in the morning and wander wild beaches by afternoon, before retreating home to your private sanctuary.
Devonport & the North-West

Only 30 minutes from Ten Fifty Bakers, Devonport is the gateway to Tasmania’s North-West Coast. Devonport won a Silver Award at the 2025 Australia’s Top Tourism Town Awards and was named the Gold Winner in 2024. It’s also home to the Spirit of Tasmania terminal and daily flights to Melbourne, perfect for seamless access to and from the island.The surrounding coast is rich with artisan producers, beaches, and natural attractions, all within an easy drive of the retreat.
Highlights Include
Ghost Rock Vineyard
Coastal cool-climate wines and a relaxed cellar-door restaurant.
Don River Railway
Heritage train rides through scenic coastal landscapes.
Hawley Beach & Port Sorell
Calm, sunlit beaches perfect for a quiet coastal escape.
Ashgrove Cheese
Award-winning artisan dairy, crafted at Elizabeth Town.
Christmas Hills Raspberry Farm
A sweet stop for fresh berries and local desserts.
Sheffield – The Town of Murals
A charming creative hub where streets and laneways are transformed into an open-air art gallery celebrating Tasmania’s history and landscape.

blocks.alt: Aerial view of two small trains stopped on railway tracks beside a river, surrounded by dense trees and greenery under a partly cloudy sky.
A rustic wooden table set for two with plates, serviettes, wine glasses filled with red wine, a bottle of wine, and a dish of roast chicken and salad near a window overlooking a garden.
A white bowl filled with several dark chocolate-covered cherries, with one cherry partially bitten to reveal the red fruit inside, sits on a wooden surface in dappled sunlight.
A mountain biker rides down a winding dirt track surrounded by tall trees in a forest under a partly cloudy sky.
Two women stand at a refrigerated cheese display in a speciality food shop, one holding a basket while the other selects cheese. Shelves and promotional signs are visible in the background.
A person stands on rocks along a sandy beach with gentle waves and a calm blue sea under a clear sky.
A woman in a hat stands in front of a mural depicting a group of horses and riders on a racecourse, painted in greyscale on a white brick wall.

blocks.text: Launceston & the Tamar Valley

Just an hour from the retreat, Launceston combines heritage charm with a globally celebrated food and wine culture. Winding north from the city, the Tamar Valley Wine Trail meanders through riverbanks and hillsides to the coast at Beauty Point, perfect for a day of tastings, cellar doors, and regional flavours.

blocks.alt: A zookeeper from Tasmania Zoo holds a small animal while speaking to a group of four people, including two children, near an outdoor enclosure on a sunny day.
A small building labelled Cellar Door stands between two tall trees, with a bar and stools visible at the entrance under a clear blue sky at Sinapius Vineyard
Two people ride a chairlift over a rocky river, suspended by cables above the water and surrounded by greenery and rocks.
Various marine animals, including a cuttlefish, seahorses, tropical fish, a snail, and a starfish, are shown underwater near the sea floor.

blocks.text: Sinapius Vineyard
Small-batch pinot noir and chardonnay at Pipers River.
Cataract Gorge Reserve
Launceston’s spectacular riverside gorge, ideal for a morning walk or picnic.
Seahorse World & Platypus House
Unique, side-by-side attractions in Beauty Point, dedicated to marine and native Tasmanian wildlife.
Tasmania Zoo
Home to Tasmanian devils and a wide variety of native and exotic species, perfect for families and wildlife lovers.
Brady’s Lookout
A classic Tamar Valley vantage point near Exeter, overlooking the river and surrounding vineyards.

blocks.alt: Four people riding horses along a sandy beach, leaving hoofprints near the shoreline, with gentle waves and distant hills in the background.

blocks.text: Explore on Horseback

For those seeking adventure without the drive, Cradle Country Adventures guided horse rides right on Bakers Beach, just minutes from Ten Fifty Bakers.Ride along the sand dunes and shoreline with views across Narawntapu National Park, a rare chance to experience Tasmania’s wilderness from a new perspective. Suitable for all experience levels, it’s a memorable way to connect with the landscape that surrounds your stay.

blocks.alt: A group of seven walkers wearing outdoor gear and rucksacks walk across a grassy field under an overcast sky.

blocks.text: Explore on Foot

For those craving to move slowly through wilderness, Bakers Walking Co offers guided walking tours departing directly from Ten Fifty Bakers. Wander along private tracks, hidden creeks, and bushland fringes, with sweeping views across Narawntapu’s rugged coastline. Every step brings you deeper into Tasmania’s silent beauty, ideal for all fitness levels, with time to breathe, listen, and reconnect. It’s a walk that lingers long after the horizon fades.

blocks.text: The prescription for a proper break in Shearwater
Where every stay is a remedy.

blocks.text: A group stay where the only thing on the to-do list is having a good time

The Prescription Pad is where the treatment plan is simple. Gather your favourite people and relax.Once a local doctor’s surgery, this Shearwater holiday house now plays host to milestone celebrations, long weekends and the kind of family holidays that are big on laughs and light on rules. With space for up to ten guests, a hot tub, pool table and the beach just minutes away, it’s the perfect location for your group stay in Tasmania, designed for connection, comfort and a little chaos.

pages.title: About The Prescription Pad | Shearwater Holiday House

blocks.text: A Holiday House with a past life
About The Prescription Pad

Before it became a place for pool comps, hot tub chats and barefoot BBQs, The Prescription Pad was an actual doctor’s surgery in Shearwater. These days it hands out a different kind of treatment plan, time with your people, space to unwind and a break from the everyday.We didn’t want to create a show home. We wanted somewhere that feels lived in and easy to be in. The kind of place where the board games are always out, the eight-ball is on rotation, adults can exhale and there’s space for everyone to truly relax.

blocks.alt: A wooden hot tub with metal bands stands on gravel, accessed by a small wooden staircase with a black handrail. There is a white wall and a white lamp in the background.

blocks.text: Our take on a good holiday

The house was redesigned with groups in mind. Two or three families. Old friends. Cousins. Celebrations. Long weekends. No stiff styling or “don’t touch that” energy. Just comfort, room to spread out and a layout that lets everyone do their thing without getting in each other’s way.We’re big believers in the kind of break where the best moments aren’t scheduled. A soak in the hot tub, a late-night pool tournament, a beach walk in thongs, a chat over breakfast that rolls into lunchtime.
Prescription: Take as needed
We’re here to give families and friends a place to reconnect without the stress. If you leave feeling lighter, closer and already planning your next stay, we’ll call that a full recovery.

blocks.text: Space for 10. Comfort for everyone.
Accommodation at The Prescription Pad

When you book The Prescription Pad, you’re booking more than just beds. You’re booking room for family chaos, space for friends to spread out, and the kind of holiday house that actually works for groups.

Whether you’re here for a milestone birthday, a long weekend with friends or the annual family getaway, The Prescription Pad is group accommodation in North West Tasmania that makes it easy to relax, connect and have fun. Here’s what makes it work so well:

pages.title: Explore Shearwater & Port Sorell | The Prescription Pad

blocks.text: Coastal calm with plenty to do
Explore Shearwater & surrounds

The Prescription Pad sits in the heart of Shearwater, a beachside town that shares the coastline with Port Sorell. It’s the kind of place where you can park the car and forget about it for a few days. Beaches are close, shops are next door and the pub is right across the road.If you want a laidback base with things to do at your own pace, this corner of the coast makes it easy.

blocks.alt: A simple black and white line drawing of a beach scene with a sun, a parasol, wavy water, and sand.

blocks.text: Beach Time
The local beaches are perfect for swimming, paddling, sandcastles or just flopping on a towel. Families love the calm water and wide shorelines, and it’s only a short walk from the house.

blocks.alt: Black and white line drawing of a kangaroo facing forward with one arm bent and its tail extended behind.

blocks.text: Wildlife & Walks
Narawntapu National Park is nearby and is known for its kangaroos, wallabies and sweeping coastal trails. It’s an easy day out that doesn’t require being a hiker.

blocks.alt: Black and white line drawing of juice bottles, a glass with a straw, a carrot, a whole and sliced citrus fruit, and a round fruit or vegetable.

blocks.text: Eat & Wander
Grab supplies from the IGA, settle in for a parmi at the pub or take your fish and chips down to the sand. Everything is close and stress-free.

blocks.alt: A narrow dirt path winds through dry shrubs on a hillside, leading toward a calm ocean under a partly cloudy sky.

blocks.text: Gateway to the North West Coast

Devonport is only about 20 minutes away, so day trips are easy. It’s home to the Spirit of Tasmania, cafes, restaurants, galleries and the Don River Railway if you’ve got train lovers in the group. If you need some retail therapy or something to do on a cooler day, it’s close while still feeling like an adventure.Staying in Shearwater puts you in a handy spot for exploring more of the region if you feel like adventuring.
Do a lot or do very little

You can swim, walk, explore, eat and day trip. Or you can sit in the hot tub, wander to the beach and call that a successful itinerary. That’s the beauty of this part of Tasmania, it works however you want to holiday.

pages.title: Refined Devonport heritage guesthouse | Annie May

blocks.text: She makes time feel unhurried and memorable
Refined stays in a Devonport heritage guesthouse
She knows how to hold a moment

Seven elegant ensuite rooms, hotel-level ease and the grace of a private residence. Soft light, quiet-close details and space that keeps the world at bay. No interruptions, no fuss, just a beautifully prepared stay that lets everything else fall away.

blocks.alt: A tan leather sofa with assorted cushions sits beside a round patterned coffee table holding a metal tray and a small dish. A black shelving unit with books and decor stands against the wall near a white fireplace.

blocks.text: Heritage kept, comforts modernised

This heritage home was renovated around how you actually live while away. Seating set for conversation. Bedside switches where your hand falls. Linens that breathe. Ensuite rooms finished with considered hardware and quiet-close everything. The balance you want; character intact, everything simply works.
Made for reconnection and retreat
For marking moments with the people you love or finding your own quiet after full days. She offers privacy with warmth, refinement without theatre.

blocks.alt: A modern bedroom with a large bed, green accents, and a throw blanket; an open door reveals a bathroom with exposed brick walls and a freestanding bath.

Breakfast Room
A light-filled room for slow starts and easy conversation.
Lift Access

Luxury Amenities

She looks after the details
You bring the moment. She brings the calm. Reserve a stay that feels effortless.

pages.title: Accommodation in Devonport | Rooms and amenities | Annie May

blocks.text: Accommodation at Annie May
How she looks after you

Evenings settle easily here. Light lands softly, doors close with a polite hush, and the house holds the pace for you. She is a heritage guesthouse shaped for calm, with the kind of finishing that disappears into comfort.

blocks.alt: A neatly made bed with white linen, three decorative cushions including a colourful patterned cushion and an orange cylindrical cushion, a blue headboard, a lamp, and potted plants on a white shelf in the foreground.
A white upholstered sofa sits in front of a window with sheer curtains; a green potted plant is placed nearby.
A neatly made bed with green headboard sits beside a white fireplace. A round mirror, vase with dried plants, and lamp decorate the room with neutral walls.

blocks.text: In-room comforts
King Superior Room with Bath
Rooms 1 & 2
$385 per night2 Adults, includes Breakfast

King bed for real rest, a proper desk when work calls, and a large TV for easy evenings. A full-length mirror and considered lighting keep things practical without fuss. The ensuite is a little retreat of its own, with a walk-in shower and a separate bath for an unhurried soak.
King Superior Room
Rooms 3 – 6
$350 per night2 Adults, includes Breakfast

Light-filled and quietly elegant. Generous space, a proper desk when you need it, a large TV for easy evenings and a full-length mirror to keep things practical. The ensuite keeps it modern and simple with a walk-in shower for an unhurried start or end to the day.
Loft Room
Room 7

Intimate and calm on the second level. A king bed for real rest, two armchairs for quiet moments, a desk when work calls and a large TV for easy evenings. A full-length mirror keeps things practical. The ensuite bathroom adds comfort with a walk-in shower and underfloor heating.

pages.title: The Annie May story | Heritage with modern ease

blocks.text: Annie May’s Story

blocks.alt: A woman in a pink shirt and light jeans sits on a bench by a window, smiling. A black console table with dried flowers and framed art is nearby.

blocks.text: The house that kept calling

For years, Deb rode past the old Formby Road house and felt something stir. Tired and a little forgotten, but quiet charm under the dust. In 2021 she stepped inside for the first time. Bunk beds from backpacker days, jackets on chairs, a room paused mid-sentence. She did not see what was missing. She saw what could be. With her husband, Craig, she brought the feeling back, slowly and lovingly, so the house could hold people again without fuss.
The name she carries

She is named for Deb’s grandmother, Annie May. The woman who never wore trousers, had her hair set every Tuesday, and kept stockings folded in neat rows. Elegant in a quiet way. Tea before you asked. A look that reminded you to sit up straight. Naming the house after her just made sense. Because like her, the house is full of warmth and grace. It holds you without fuss. It knows beauty is in the little things. This is a homemade recipe to make you feel looked after. Just like Annie May always did.

blocks.alt: A neatly made bed with white linen, three decorative cushions including a colourful patterned cushion and an orange cylindrical cushion, a blue headboard, a lamp, and potted plants on a white shelf in the foreground.
A woman in a pink shirt and jeans stands smiling by a black stairway in a well-lit, modern hallway with wooden floors and decorative items.

pages.title: Explore Devonport and North West Tasmania | Annie May

blocks.text: Explore
Her location is central

Annie May sits in the sweet spot for exploring Devonport and beyond. She is minutes from the Spirit of Tasmania terminal and Devonport Airport, an easy walk to the city for dining and events, and a refined base for coastal drives and North West adventures. Think of her as your gateway to Tasmania, with everything close and the pace kept calm.

blocks.alt: A neatly made bed with white linen, three decorative cushions including a colourful patterned cushion and an orange cylindrical cushion, a blue headboard, a lamp, and potted plants on a white shelf in the foreground.

blocks.text: Devonport & the North-West

Here are the places she sends her favourite guests. From coastal walks and riverfront paths to good coffee, and easy dinner spots, these highlights are close enough to wander to and worth a short drive if you feel like exploring. Start nearby, then let the day stretch.
Highlights Include
Ghost Rock Vineyard
Coastal cool-climate wines and a relaxed cellar-door restaurant.
The Tasmania Arboretum
A peaceful sanctuary of trees, lakes, and quiet paths, perfect for a slow afternoon and the chance to spot a platypus.
Ashgrove Cheese
Award-winning artisan dairy, crafted at Elizabeth Town.
Bakers Walking Co

Guided day walks from Ten Fifty Bakers, exploring private tracks, wildlife-rich bushland, and coastal views across a 1,137-acre wilderness beside Narawntapu National Park. With easy to moderate terrain, small groups, and a gourmet Tasmanian lunch included, it’s a five-hour nature experience designed to help you slow down and reconnect. Learn More
Forth Falls walking track
A short, scenic walk through lush bushland, the Forth Falls Track rewards with cascading waterfalls and quiet moments in nature.
From Sky to Sea light show
From Sky to Sea is a captivating light show in Devonport that illuminates the city’s stories with colour, sound, and movement across buildings and sky.
Sheffield – The Town of Murals
A charming creative hub where streets and laneways are transformed into an open-air art gallery celebrating Tasmania’s history and landscape.
Southern Wild Distillery
Crafting award-winning spirits in Devonport, blending wild Tasmanian botanicals with precision and passion in every drop.

blocks.alt: A bottle of Ghost Rock Tasmania Pinot Noir 2023, a glass of red wine, a water glass, and a plate with cheese and nuts on a table.
Curved gravel and mulch path winding through a landscaped garden with various shrubs and trees, bordered by a forest in the background at the Tasmanian Arboretum.

A group of hikers with rucksacks walking across a grassy field on a cloudy day.

A modern distillery bar with shelves of bottles behind a wooden counter lat Southern Wild Distillery.
A woman wearing a hat stands in front of a mural depicting horses and riders on a racecourse, painted in black and white on a brick wall in Sheffield, Tasmania.
